1: A type: the tabernacle covered with ten curtains of fine twisted linen, blue, purple, and scarlet yarns — the interior covering of the sanctuary; what the worshiper inside sees. Fine linen = righteousness (Revelation 19:8 “fine linen is the righteous deeds of the saints”); the inside of Christ’s church is clothed in righteousness, beautiful before God even if the exterior is plain (Psalms 45:13 “all glorious within”).
2: A type: each curtain 28 cubits long and 4 cubits wide, all the same dimensions — uniformity and unity; the ten curtains are identical and join to form one covering. Ephesians 2:21: “the whole structure, being joined together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord”; the church is built from equal members, each proportioned to the same standard of grace (Ephesians 4:7, 16).
3: A type: five curtains joined to each other, and five curtains joined to each other — two sets of five; the tabernacle covering is assembled from pre-made groups. The church is organized and assembled before being joined into the whole; the Spirit knits together communities that are then united into the one body (1 Corinthians 12:27–28).
4: A type: loops of blue on the edge of each curtain for coupling — the attachment mechanism is blue, the heavenly color. The joining of the curtains is a heavenly act; the unity of the church is not human organizational achievement but Spirit-given (Ephesians 4:3 “eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace”).
5: A type: fifty loops on one curtain and fifty on the edge of the other, so that the loops correspond — perfect alignment, loop to loop; the joining requires that the two halves correspond exactly. The NT church’s unity is not forced compromise but the correspondence of those who share one Spirit, one Lord, one faith (Ephesians 4:4–6).
6: A type: fifty clasps of gold coupling the curtains “so that the tabernacle may be a single whole.” The tabernacle’s unity is the explicit theological point of the coupling; the gold clasps signal that this unity is holy and divine, not merely structural. Ephesians 4:3: “eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace”; John 17:21: “that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you.”
7: A type: curtains of goats’ hair for a tent over the tabernacle — the second layer covering the first; goats are the primary sin-offering animal (Leviticus 16:8–10); the goat-hair layer is the atonement covering over the beauty of the first curtains. Christ’s atonement covers the church: “love covers all offenses” (Proverbs 10:12); the goat-hair covering pictures the sin-bearing work of Christ that wraps his people.
8: A type: eleven curtains of goats’ hair, each 30 × 4 cubits — one more curtain than the inner set (ten), and longer; the atonement layer is greater than and encompasses the inner layer. The scope of Christ’s atonement exceeds the scope of sin: “where sin increased, grace abounded all the more” (Romans 5:20). The outer covering is larger than what it covers.
9: A type: the sixth curtain doubled at the front of the tent — the extra curtain folded back at the entrance; provision for the entry point. Christ’s atoning work makes special provision for entry: the way into the Father’s presence is not left uncovered; the entry is guarded and graced by atonement (Hebrews 10:19–22).
10: A type: fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain of the first set and on the edge of the second set — the joining loops of the outer curtains correspond to the inner ones; the atonement layer is joined by the same principle of correspondence as the first layer. The Spirit’s work of atonement-application and the Spirit’s work of church-unity operate on the same principle: divine correspondence.
11: A type: fifty bronze clasps to couple the tent together as one whole — bronze clasps for the outer tent (compared to gold clasps for the inner); bronze in the tabernacle typology carries the connotation of judgment and endurance (the bronze altar, the bronze sea). The atonement layer is joined by judgment-enduring clasps; the unity of Christ’s atoning work rests on his having endured the full weight of divine judgment.
12: A type: the extra half curtain hanging over the back of the tabernacle — the goat-hair tent’s extra length drapes down behind; complete coverage with surplus. 2 Corinthians 5:21: “for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.” The coverage of Christ’s atonement exceeds what is needed; there is no uncovered corner.
13: A type: the cubit of surplus on either side of the tabernacle length — the covering hangs over on both sides. The scope of the gospel extends beyond any single nation or people; “go and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:19); the atoning curtain covers the whole world (1 John 2:2 “he is the propitiation... also for the sins of the whole world”).
14: A type: the outer covering of tanned rams’ skins and goatskins — the tabernacle’s outermost surface was coarse animal hide, plain and unimpressive. Isaiah 53:2: “he had no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him.” Christ’s outward appearance did not advertise the glory within; the tabernacle’s unappealing exterior hid unspeakable divine glory inside.
15: A type: frames (qerāšîm) of acacia wood, upright — the structural skeleton of the tabernacle; the incorruptible desert hardwood overlaid with gold (v29) forms the standing framework. The resurrection body is the antitype of the upright, incorruptible frame: “it is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power” (1 Corinthians 15:43). The church stands upright because Christ rose upright.
16: A type: each frame 10 × 1.5 cubits, with two tenons for joining — the standardized frame dimensions and the tenons (yādôt, hands/projections) for fitting into the sockets. The church’s members are individually proportioned (each 10 × 1.5) yet built for connection; “each part is working properly” (Ephesians 4:16); every believer is shaped for articulation within the whole body.
17: A type: two tenons on each frame for joining — the double attachment point; each frame is secured at two points, not one. The believer’s security in Christ is double: “my sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand... and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father’s hand” (John 10:27–29) — the Son’s hand and the Father’s hand, two tenons.
18: A type: twenty frames for the south side of the tabernacle — the long south wall; the tabernacle’s structure is made of many individual pieces held together. The church is built of many individuals, each a framing member: “you yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house” (1 Peter 2:5). No single stone is the whole building; all are needed.
19: A type: forty silver sockets (ʾadānîm) under the twenty frames, two sockets under each frame — the foundation of the tabernacle. The silver sockets were cast from the atonement silver collected in the census (Exodus 38:25–27); the entire tabernacle structure literally stands on atonement money. The church stands on the atoning work of Christ alone: “no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ” (1 Corinthians 3:11).
20: A type: twenty frames for the north side and forty silver sockets — mirror of the south side; the whole tabernacle is symmetrical, built on equal atonement. North and south, all nations, all equally founded on atonement: “there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him” (Romans 10:12).
21: A type: forty silver sockets, two under each frame, for the north side as for the south — the atonement foundation is consistent throughout, no weak section. “Christ died for our sins” (1 Corinthians 15:3) — the atonement is not partial or approximate; the foundation is solid throughout, every socket of pure atonement-silver.
22: A type: six frames for the rear (west) end of the tabernacle — the back wall; the tabernacle is a three-sided structure open to the east. The west/back of the tabernacle is the direction of the Most Holy Place; the direction toward which worship moves — deeper in, further in, into the presence. Hebrews 10:22: “let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith.”
23: A type: two frames for the corners of the rear — the corner frames completing the structure; the corners where the long walls meet the back wall. Christ is the cornerstone: “the stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone” (Psalms 118:22; Matthew 21:42; Ephesians 2:20). The entire structure holds together at the corners; the entire church holds together in Christ the cornerstone.
24: A type: the corner frames joined together at the top with a single ring — the doubled corners, joined at the top, make a single corner unit. The unity of the church at every corner, in every direction — east-west, north-south — flows from the single ring at the top: the headship of Christ, from whom the whole body “is joined and held together” (Ephesians 4:16).
25: A type: eight frames total for the west end with sixteen silver sockets — two sockets per frame throughout. The completed rear of the tabernacle: the sanctuary enclosed on three sides, open toward those approaching from the east. Christ’s atonement establishes the complete enclosure of his people — surrounded by grace on every side (Psalms 125:2 “as the mountains surround Jerusalem, so YHWH surrounds his people”).
26: A type: crossbars (berîḥîm) of acacia wood, five for each side of the tabernacle — horizontal bars running through rings on the frames to give lateral stability. The horizontal structural members hold the vertical frames together; in the church, shared doctrine and fellowship (Acts 2:42) provide the horizontal bonds that prevent the individual members from toppling. Christ is the common truth that holds all members in coherent structure (Ephesians 4:14–15).
27: A type: five crossbars for the south side, five for the north side, five for the west — thirty bars total, the same incorruptible acacia wood overlaid with gold. The thoroughness of structural provision: no side is left unbraced. The church is held together on every side, in every direction; “the gates of hell shall not prevail against it” (Matthew 16:18) because Christ’s structural support is thorough.
28: A type: the middle crossbar runs through the frames from end to end — the central bar that traverses the entire length of the tabernacle wall. Christ as the one who runs through all of Scripture and holds the whole structure together from beginning to end: “in him all things hold together (synestēken)” (Colossians 1:17). He is the middle crossbar of all of redemptive history.
29: A type: frames overlaid with gold, rings of gold, crossbars overlaid with gold — the entire structural skeleton is gold-covered, not merely the decorative elements. The church’s foundational structure — its doctrine, its ministry, its ordinances — is holy throughout, not merely adorned on the surface. Christ’s righteousness covers the whole church, not just its visible acts (Galatians 3:27 “put on Christ”).
30: A type: “Then you shall erect the tabernacle according to the plan for it that you were shown on the mountain.” The construction must conform to the heavenly design; human initiative cannot substitute for divine pattern. The NT church is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets (Ephesians 2:20), not on human religious innovation; Christ himself is both the architect and the cornerstone of the only legitimate building plan.
31: A fulfillment: the veil (pārōket) of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen with cherubim embroidered — separating the holy place from the most holy place. This veil is the most Christologically laden object in the OT: at Christ’s death “the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom” (Matthew 27:51). Hebrews 10:19–20: “we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh.” The torn veil = Christ’s flesh torn at the crucifixion, opening unrestricted access to the Father.
32: A type: four pillars of acacia overlaid with gold on four silver sockets for hanging the veil — the veil is suspended, not fixed to a wall; it hangs between the two rooms. The veil’s hanging nature signals its temporariness: it was always meant to be removed when the permanent entered. Hebrews 9:8: “the Holy Spirit is indicating this, that the way into the holy places is not yet opened as long as the first section is still standing.”
33: A fulfillment: “You shall hang the veil from the clasps and bring the ark of the testimony in there behind the veil” — the veil divides holy from most holy, and placed the ark and mercy seat in the restricted inner sanctum. Hebrews 9:7–8: only the high priest entered, once a year, with blood. Hebrews 10:19–22: Christ opened the new and living way through the veil for all who believe — the barrier of restricted access is permanently removed.
34: A type: “You shall put the mercy seat (kappōret) on the ark of the testimony in the most holy place.” The most holy place and its mercy seat: the climax of the entire tabernacle’s typological system. Christ entered the greater and more perfect tent (Hebrews 9:11) “not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption” (Hebrews 9:12). He is in the true most holy place now.
35: A type: the table outside the veil and the lampstand opposite the table in the holy place — the daily priestly service area, where all priests could minister; the table and lampstand are for regular access. 1 Peter 2:9: “you are a royal priesthood” — all believers have the table (Eucharist) and the light (gospel witness) as their ongoing priestly service. The inner sanctum is now open (Hebrews 4:16); the daily service area is for all.
36: A type: a screen (māsāk) for the entrance to the tent — the doorway into the holy place; the screen is more accessible than the veil but still marks the sacred threshold. Christ: “I am the door. If anyone enters by me, he will be saved and will go in and out and find pasture” (John 10:9). The screen-entrance pictures Christ as the single legitimate gateway into the Father’s presence.
37: A type: five pillars of acacia overlaid with gold for the screen, with golden hooks and five bronze sockets — entry pillars with bronze (judgment) bases and golden (grace) tops; to enter the holy place, one must pass from the judgment-foundation to the grace of the inner sanctuary. The gospel proclaims the same movement: from the bronze altar of judgment-bearing (the cross) to the golden holy place (union with Christ). The way in runs through the judgment Christ bore.
